**East Stirlingshire Football Club** is a [Scottish](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the town of [Falkirk](/wiki/Falkirk "Falkirk"). The club was founded in 1881 and competes in the [Lowland League](/wiki/Lowland_Football_League "Lowland Football League"), the fifth tier of the [Scottish football league system](/wiki/Scottish_football_league_system "Scottish football league system"). The club's origins can be traced to 1880 when a local cricket club formed a football team under the name *Bainsford Britannia*.[[1]](#cite_note-ES_History-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FH_Shire_Mettle-2)

The club was elected to the [Scottish Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League "Scottish Football League") in [1900–01](/wiki/1900%E2%80%9301_in_Scottish_football "1900–01 in Scottish football") and has competed in the league system for most of its existence. East Stirlingshire has [won the second tier](/wiki/List_of_winners_of_the_Scottish_First_Division_and_predecessors "List of winners of the Scottish First Division and predecessors") of Scottish football once and finished runners-up once, earning promotion to the top-flight on both occasions. The club's highest league ranking came during the two solitary seasons it competed in the top flight in [1932–33](/wiki/1932%E2%80%9333_in_Scottish_football "1932–33 in Scottish football") and [1963–64](/wiki/1963%E2%80%9364_in_Scottish_football "1963–64 in Scottish football"). In 2016, East Stirlingshire became the first club ever to be relegated out of the national league system.

East Stirlingshire first entered in the [Scottish Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Cup "Scottish Cup") in [1882](/wiki/1882%E2%80%9383_Scottish_Cup "1882–83 Scottish Cup"), its best result reaching the quarter-finals on three occasions, the last in [1981](/wiki/1980%E2%80%9381_Scottish_Cup "1980–81 Scottish Cup"). The club's best result in a national cup competition was in the [2000–01 season](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Scottish_Challenge_Cup "2000–01 Scottish Challenge Cup") when it reached the semi-finals of the [Scottish Challenge Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Challenge_Cup "Scottish Challenge Cup"), losing to [Livingston](/wiki/Livingston_F.C. "Livingston F.C.") for a place in the final.[[3]](#cite_note-BBC_Livingston_preview-3) In [2008](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_in_Scottish_football "2007–08 in Scottish football"), the club left [Firs Park](/wiki/Firs_Park "Firs Park") and moved to [Ochilview Park](/wiki/Ochilview_Park "Ochilview Park") to [ground-share](/wiki/Groundshare "Groundshare") with local rivals [Stenhousemuir](/wiki/Stenhousemuir_F.C. "Stenhousemuir F.C.").[[4]](#cite_note-History_2000-Present-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Firs_Park_departure-5) In 2018 they moved to groundshare with [Falkirk](/wiki/Falkirk_F.C. "Falkirk F.C.") at [Falkirk Stadium](/wiki/Falkirk_Stadium "Falkirk Stadium") until returning to Ochilview for the [2025–26 season](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_in_Scottish_football "2025–26 in Scottish football").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Stirlingshire_F.C.&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of East Stirlingshire F.C.](/wiki/History_of_East_Stirlingshire_F.C. "History of East Stirlingshire F.C.")

See also: [List of East Stirlingshire F.C. seasons](/wiki/List_of_East_Stirlingshire_F.C._seasons "List of East Stirlingshire F.C. seasons")

East Stirlingshire's official date of formation was in 1881,[[7]](#cite_note-East_Stirlingshire_SPFL-7) but its origins can be traced to the previous year when a local cricket club called Bainsford Bluebonnets formed a football team under the name Britannia.[[8]](#cite_note-History_1880-1900-8) The team's first recorded match was a [friendly](/wiki/Exhibition_match "Exhibition match") against [Falkirk](/wiki/Falkirk_F.C. "Falkirk F.C.")'s second eleven in December 1880 and resulted in a 7–0 defeat while its first match as East Stirlingshire was against the same opponent in August 1881.[[9]](#cite_note-Falkirk_FC_matches-9) The club's nickname is "The Shire", which refers to the Stirlingshire part of the club name.[[7]](#cite_note-East_Stirlingshire_SPFL-7) After joining the [Scottish Football Association](/wiki/Scottish_Football_Association "Scottish Football Association"), the club became eligible to compete in the [Scottish Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Cup "Scottish Cup"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland")'s main association football [knock-out competition](/wiki/Single-elimination_tournament "Single-elimination tournament"), eliminated in its first game in 1882. In December 1883, the Stirlingshire Football Association was founded, with membership open to clubs exclusively from the county of [Stirlingshire](/wiki/Stirlingshire "Stirlingshire"). It resulted in the establishment of a new regional tournament called the [Stirlingshire Cup](/wiki/Stirlingshire_Cup "Stirlingshire Cup"), with the club reaching the final in its first season.[[10]](#cite_note-Stirlingshire_Cup-10) East Stirlingshire dominated the tournament in its early years, winning it for a record four consecutive seasons 91885 and 18890,[[10]](#cite_note-Stirlingshire_Cup-10) including an emphatic 9–0 victory against Falkirk in the 1888 final.[[11]](#cite_note-Stirlingshire_Cup_Final_1888-11) Two goals came from Lawrence McLachlan who was an influential goalscorer in the club's early successes; scoring more times than any other East Stirlingshire player with 135 known goals.[[8]](#cite_note-History_1880-1900-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/09/East_Stirlingshire_F.C._team_1891.jpg/330px-East_Stirlingshire_F.C._team_1891.jpg)](/wiki/File:East_Stirlingshire_F.C._team_1891.jpg)

East Stirlingshire squad in [1891](/wiki/1890%E2%80%9391_in_Scottish_football "1890–91 in Scottish football") with several trophies won by the club including the [Stirlingshire Cup](/wiki/Stirlingshire_Cup "Stirlingshire Cup").

The latter years of the 19th century was East Stirlingshire's most successful era in the Scottish Cup. In the [1888–89](/wiki/1888%E2%80%9389_Scottish_Cup "1888–89 Scottish Cup") and [1890–91](/wiki/1890%E2%80%9391_Scottish_Cup "1890–91 Scottish Cup") tournaments, the club reached the quarter-finals in what was to be the last time for 91 years, losing to [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C.") and [Hearts](/wiki/Heart_of_Midlothian_F.C. "Heart of Midlothian F.C.") respectively.[[8]](#cite_note-History_1880-1900-8) In the 1888–89 tournament, the club recorded its highest win and equalled it in successive rounds with a 10–1 victory against local rivals [Stenhousemuir](/wiki/Stenhousemuir_F.C. "Stenhousemuir F.C.") in the first round and an 11–2 win over [Vale of Bannock](/wiki/Vale_of_Bannock_F.C. "Vale of Bannock F.C.") in the second round. It was during this period that four East Stirlingshire players earned international caps for their countries. The first was the [Wales national team](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") captain, [Humphrey Jones](/wiki/Humphrey_Jones "Humphrey Jones"), who earned five caps whilst with the club. Three other players, [David Alexander](/wiki/David_Alexander_(footballer) "David Alexander (footballer)"), [Archibald Ritchie](/wiki/Archibald_Ritchie_(footballer,_born_1872) "Archibald Ritchie (footballer, born 1872)"), and [James McKee](/wiki/James_McKee_(footballer) "James McKee (footballer)") made appearances for the [Scotland national team](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") from 1891 to 1898.[[12]](#cite_note-London_Hearts_Shire_Scotland-12)

Between 1891 and 1899, the club competed in regional leagues, including winning the [Midland Football League](/wiki/Midland_Football_League_in_Scotland "Midland Football League in Scotland")[[13]](#cite_note-SFHA_Midland_League-13) and [Central Football Combination](/wiki/Central_Football_Combination "Central Football Combination"),[[14]](#cite_note-SFHA_Central_Combination-14) before being elected to the [second tier](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Division_Two "Scottish Football League Division Two") of Scotland's main national league competition – the [Scottish Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League "Scottish Football League") – in [1900–01](/wiki/1900%E2%80%9301_in_Scottish_football "1900–01 in Scottish football"), ending its first season ranked 7th from ten.[[15]](#cite_note-SFL_Elections-15)[[16]](#cite_note-FCHD_East_Stirlingshire-16) In March 1905, a proposal was raised for the club to merge with neighbours Falkirk with an aim to creating a bigger and more financially stable club, which Falkirk accepted in a vote. However, East Stirlingshire's vote was not in favour and the club rejected the proposal.[[17]](#cite_note-Better_meddle_Falkirk_merger-17) The years following included finishing bottom of the league in [1905–06](/wiki/1905%E2%80%9306_in_Scottish_football "1905–06 in Scottish football"), recording only one win from 22 games, contrasting with finishing as high as third in table in [1912–13](/wiki/1912%E2%80%9313_in_Scottish_football "1912–13 in Scottish football"), narrowly missing out on winning the championship by two points.[[16]](#cite_note-FCHD_East_Stirlingshire-16)[[18]](#cite_note-SFL_League_table_1912/13-18) The club remained in Division Two until [1914–15](/wiki/1914%E2%80%9315_in_Scottish_football "1914–15 in Scottish football") when it, and the [Scottish Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Cup "Scottish Cup"), were both suspended due to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").[[19]](#cite_note-Scottish_FA_Cup_History-19)

[![refer to caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/bc/East_Stirlingshire_FC_league_rankings_1900_to_2012.png/330px-East_Stirlingshire_FC_league_rankings_1900_to_2012.png)](/wiki/File:East_Stirlingshire_FC_league_rankings_1900_to_2012.png)

Chart showing the progress of East Stirlingshire F.C. through the [Scottish football league system](/wiki/Scottish_football_league_system "Scottish football league system") from [1900–01](/wiki/1900%E2%80%9301_in_Scottish_football "1900–01 in Scottish football") to [2011–12](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_in_Scottish_football "2011–12 in Scottish football")

At the end of World War I, the club was re-elected to the old Division Two which was re-established in the [1921–22 season](/wiki/1921%E2%80%9322_in_Scottish_football "1921–22 in Scottish football").[[15]](#cite_note-SFL_Elections-15) The year beforehand saw the club move to [Firs Park](/wiki/Firs_Park "Firs Park") after leaving their old ground of [Merchiston Park](/wiki/Merchiston_Park "Merchiston Park") in 1920. In the same year, a [record home attendance](/wiki/Scottish_football_attendance_records "Scottish football attendance records") of 12,000 was set when the club played eventual champions, [Partick Thistle](/wiki/Partick_Thistle_F.C. "Partick Thistle F.C."), in the Scottish Cup in February 1921. The club was relegated to the newly created, but short lived, [Division Three](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Division_Three "Scottish Football League Division Three") in [1922–23](/wiki/1922%E2%80%9323_in_Scottish_football "1922–23 in Scottish football"), earning promotion back to Division Two after one season; setting a record of 23 home games without a loss.[[20]](#cite_note-statto_records-20) A decade later, East Stirlingshire won promotion to [Division One](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Division_One "Scottish Football League Division One"), Scotland's [top flight](/wiki/Scottish_football_league_system "Scottish football league system"), for the first time. En route to promotion, the club spent 32 weeks at the top of Division Two, ending the season equal on 55 points with [St Johnstone](/wiki/St_Johnstone_F.C. "St Johnstone F.C."), with East Stirlingshire winning the championship on a superior [goal average](/wiki/Goal_average "Goal average").[[21]](#cite_note-Statto_1931-32-21) East Stirlingshire spent one season in its first spell in the top flight, ending the year bottom of the league in 20th with seventeen points.[[22]](#cite_note-LondonHearts_1932-33-22) Back in Division Two in the [1935–36 season](/wiki/1935%E2%80%9336_in_Scottish_football "1935–36 in Scottish football"), the club's heaviest league defeat of 12–1 was inflicted by [Dundee United](/wiki/Dundee_United_F.C. "Dundee United F.C.") in April 1936.[[20]](#cite_note-statto_records-20)[[23]](#cite_note-ArabArchive-23) In [1938–39](/wiki/1938%E2%80%9339_in_Scottish_football "1938–39 in Scottish football"), the final season before the league was suspended due to [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), East Stirlingshire finished second-bottom of the league, ahead of only [Edinburgh City](/wiki/Edinburgh_City_F.C. "Edinburgh City F.C."), but despite finishing low, Malcolm Morrison became the club's highest league goalscorer in a single season with 36 goals.[[24]](#cite_note-Soccerbase_records-24)

The club was excluded from a [wartime league](/wiki/Association_football_during_World_War_II "Association football during World War II") in 1939 and was also denied re-entry to the Scottish Football League Division Two at the end of World War II, along with six other small league clubs.[[15]](#cite_note-SFL_Elections-15)[[25]](#cite_note-SFL_Division_C-25) As a result, the clubs competed in a newly created [Division C](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Division_C "Scottish Football League Division C") along with the [reserve teams](/wiki/Reserve_team "Reserve team") of some of Scotland's top clubs. In the second season in Division C, East Stirlingshire won the league and was promoted back to Division Two, now renamed "Division B", but finished last after one season in [1948–49](/wiki/1948%E2%80%9349_in_Scottish_football "1948–49 in Scottish football"), and were relegated back to the "C Division".

After another six seasons competing in "Division C" the club rejoined Division Two in [1955–56](/wiki/1955%E2%80%9356_in_Scottish_football "1955–56 in Scottish football") when the " C Division" was abolished and the number of teams in the Scottish Football League was expanded. The club sold many young players to larger clubs, including [defender](/wiki/Defender_(association_football) "Defender (association football)") [Eddie McCreadie](/wiki/Eddie_McCreadie "Eddie McCreadie") to [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") in 1962 who earned 23 international caps for Scotland and is tenth in Chelsea's all-time appearance list with 410.[[26]](#cite_note-SFA_McCreadie-26)[[27]](#cite_note-First_Touch_McCreadie-27) The money earned from the transfer helped attract new players and in the [1962–63 season](/wiki/1962%E2%80%9363_in_Scottish_football "1962–63 in Scottish football"), the club won promotion to the top flight for a second time, as runners-up in Division Two behind St Johnstone.[[28]](#cite_note-History_1940-1970-28) Like the previous occasion in [1932–33](/wiki/1932%E2%80%9333_in_Scottish_football "1932–33 in Scottish football"), East Stirlingshire spent one solitary season in the top tier before being relegated.

After relegation from Scotland's top flight, East Stirlingshire was controversially merged with [Clydebank Juniors](/wiki/Clydebank_Juniors_F.C. "Clydebank Juniors F.C.") in [1964](/wiki/1964%E2%80%9365_in_Scottish_football "1964–65 in Scottish football"). The new club was renamed *East Stirlingshire Clydebank* – often E.S. Clydebank for short – and relocated to [Kilbowie Park](/wiki/Kilbowie_Park "Kilbowie Park") in [Clydebank](/wiki/Clydebank "Clydebank") against fans' wishes.[[28]](#cite_note-History_1940-1970-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Historical_Kits_Clydebank-29) However, the merge lasted only one season, with East Stirlingshire [shareholders](/wiki/Shareholder "Shareholder") winning several court cases against it and thus the club reverted to its original legal status and moved back to [Falkirk](/wiki/Falkirk "Falkirk").[[30]](#cite_note-SFHA_Directory-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Historical_Kits_East_Stirlingshire-31)[[32]](#cite_note-BBC_Bankies-32) During the single season as E.S. Clydebank, the club set a [record attendance](/wiki/Scottish_football_attendance_records "Scottish football attendance records") at Kilbowie Park when 14,900 spectators attended a Scottish Cup first round replay against [Hibernian](/wiki/Hibernian_F.C. "Hibernian F.C.") in February 1965.[[33]](#cite_note-ESClydebank_programmes-33)[[34]](#cite_note-ESClydebank_Hibernian-34)

In 1966, [Lawrence Binnie](/wiki/Lawrence_Binnie "Lawrence Binnie") became the club's first official [manager](/wiki/Coach_(sport) "Coach (sport)"), prior to his appointment the board of directors took responsibility for management roles.[[28]](#cite_note-History_1940-1970-28) After de-merging with Clydebank Juniors, the club spent ten seasons in Division Two before being moved by league reconstruction to a new [Second Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Second_Division "Scottish Football League Second Division") for the [1975–76 season](/wiki/1975%E2%80%9376_in_Scottish_football "1975–76 in Scottish football"), now the third tier with the creation of the [Premier Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Premier_Division "Scottish Football League Premier Division") at the top of the Scottish Football League. During this period, the club progressed past the group stage of [Scottish League Cup](/wiki/Scottish_League_Cup "Scottish League Cup") for the first time since it was inaugurated in 1947.[[35]](#cite_note-Shire_League_Cup_LondonHearts-35) The year beforehand saw the club appoint its most famous manager, [Alex Ferguson](/wiki/Alex_Ferguson "Alex Ferguson"), who was at the club during 1974 before moving to [St Mirren](/wiki/St_Mirren_F.C. "St Mirren F.C.").[[36]](#cite_note-History_1970-2000-36) In [1979–80](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_in_Scottish_football "1979–80 in Scottish football"), East Stirlingshire won promotion to the [First Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_First_Division "Scottish Football League First Division"), which is the last time the club was promoted in the league. Managed by [Billy Lamont](/wiki/Billy_Lamont "Billy Lamont"), the club finished runners-up by one point to rivals [Falkirk](/wiki/Falkirk_F.C. "Falkirk F.C.") in the Second Division.[[36]](#cite_note-History_1970-2000-36)[[37]](#cite_note-SFL_League_table_1979/80-37) The clubs shared the top two spots in the league for most of the season and entered the final match equal on points, with East Stirlingshire required to better Falkirk's result to win the championship due to an inferior goal difference. However, Falkirk won its match and East Stirlingshire drew and so finished second.[[36]](#cite_note-History_1970-2000-36)

[![Photograph of players on a football pitch taken from a spectator stand.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/EastStirlingvDundeeUnited-20090111.jpg/250px-EastStirlingvDundeeUnited-20090111.jpg)](/wiki/File:EastStirlingvDundeeUnited-20090111.jpg)

Warm up before a [Scottish Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Cup "Scottish Cup") match between East Stirlingshire and [Dundee United](/wiki/Dundee_United_F.C. "Dundee United F.C.") at [Ochilview Park](/wiki/Ochilview_Park "Ochilview Park") in 2009

In the [1994–95 season](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_in_Scottish_football "1994–95 in Scottish football"), the Scottish Football League was again restructured and East Stirlingshire was moved to a new [Third Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Third_Division "Scottish Football League Third Division"), now the fourth-tier. The club finished its first season in the Third Division ranked fourth from ten and repeated it again in [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_in_Scottish_football "1997–98 in Scottish football"). With these exceptions East Stirlingshire generally finished below mid-table, including a final day victory against [Arbroath](/wiki/Arbroath_F.C. "Arbroath F.C.") in the [1996–97 season](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_in_Scottish_football "1996–97 in Scottish football") to avoid finishing last. In [2000–01](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_in_Scottish_football "2000–01 in Scottish football"), the club reached the semi-final of a national cup competition for the first time in its history, losing 2–1 to [Livingston](/wiki/Livingston_F.C. "Livingston F.C.") for a place in the final of the [Scottish Challenge Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Challenge_Cup "Scottish Challenge Cup").[[3]](#cite_note-BBC_Livingston_preview-3)[[38]](#cite_note-BBC_Livingston_result-38)

At the start of the 21st century the club struggled financially, paying players [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")10-a-week and manager Dennis Newall became the first unpaid manager at senior level in Scottish football.[[39]](#cite_note-BBC_Boss_nothing-39) As a result, the club ranked last in the Scottish Football League for five consecutive seasons from [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_in_Scottish_football "2002–03 in Scottish football") to [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_in_Scottish_football "2006–07 in Scottish football"), losing 24 consecutive league games in the [2003–04 season](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_in_Scottish_football "2003–04 in Scottish football"), recording only 8 points from two wins and two draws in 36 games.[[40]](#cite_note-statto_results_2003/04-40)[[41]](#cite_note-BBC_windfall-41)[[42]](#cite_note-BBC_Elgin25-42) From the [2005–06 season](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_in_Scottish_football "2005–06 in Scottish football"), it was determined if a club finished bottom of the Third Division twice in successive years, it would face the risk of being reduced to associate member status, meaning the loss of voting rights.[[43]](#cite_note-BBC_uncertain_future-43) In East Stirlingshire's case, a league vote was held and concluded in favour of the club retaining full member status with the condition that it did not finish last the following season in 2008.[[44]](#cite_note-BBC_year_to_improve-44) The club won its final game of the season to move off the bottom of the table in a 3–1 win against [Montrose](/wiki/Montrose_F.C. "Montrose F.C."), which was also the last game at [Firs Park](/wiki/Firs_Park "Firs Park").[[45]](#cite_note-BBC_3-1_Montrose-45) The following season, retaining full member status, the club moved to [Ochilview Park](/wiki/Ochilview_Park "Ochilview Park") in a [ground-share](/wiki/Groundshare "Groundshare") agreement with neighbours [Stenhousemuir](/wiki/Stenhousemuir_F.C. "Stenhousemuir F.C.").

Under manager [Jim McInally](/wiki/Jim_McInally "Jim McInally"), the club finished third in successive years to qualify for the [promotion play-offs](/wiki/Playoffs#Scotland "Playoffs") to the [Second Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Second_Division#Introduction_of_Play-Offs "Scottish Football League Second Division"). However, a second-bottom place finish in the 2010–11 season saw McInally sacked and replaced by [John Coughlin](/wiki/John_Coughlin_(footballer) "John Coughlin (footballer)"). The club would subsequently post another two bottom-place finishes in the seasons to follow, which could have again put them in danger of reduction to associate member status, but due to ongoing negotiations about league restructuring for the [2013–14 season](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_in_Scottish_football "2013–14 in Scottish football"), the league opted not to enforce the rule. The negotiations ultimately saw the formation of a new governing body, the [Scottish Professional Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Professional_Football_League "Scottish Professional Football League"), following the merger of the Scottish Football League and [Scottish Premier League](/wiki/Scottish_Premier_League "Scottish Premier League"). East Stirlingshire joined the [Scottish League Two](/wiki/Scottish_League_Two "Scottish League Two"), the successor to the old Third Division.[[46]](#cite_note-BBC_new_leagues-46) In October 2015, on loan [Raith Rovers](/wiki/Raith_Rovers_F.C. "Raith Rovers F.C.") forward Jonny Court scored the [SPFL](/wiki/Scottish_Professional_Football_League "Scottish Professional Football League")'s 5000th goal (across all four divisions, but not including [play-offs](/wiki/Play-offs "Play-offs")), in a 2–0 home win over [Elgin City](/wiki/Elgin_City_F.C. "Elgin City F.C.").[[47]](#cite_note-47) Having finished bottom of [2015–16 Scottish League Two](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Scottish_League_Two "2015–16 Scottish League Two"), East Stirlingshire faced a play-off against [Edinburgh City](/wiki/Edinburgh_City_F.C. "Edinburgh City F.C.") for a place in [2016–17 Scottish League Two](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Scottish_League_Two "2016–17 Scottish League Two"). Edinburgh City won 2–1 on aggregate, relegating East Stirlingshire to the [Lowland League](/wiki/Lowland_Football_League "Lowland Football League") and ending their 61-year tenure in the Scottish national league set-up.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Black and white hoops from 1882

East Stirlingshire's home colours have been black and white hoops for most of its existence since 1882.[[8]](#cite_note-History_1880-1900-8) The club's first strip was thin blue and white hoops between 1880 and 1882, before changing to colours similar to the present day. An all-black shirt was introduced between 1960 and 1962, and again between 2008 and 2010, while the club's centenary strip in 1981 was all-white. In [1996–97](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_in_Scottish_football "1996–97 in Scottish football"), the stripes were changed from horizontal to vertical for a full season; a similar design featured for one month in August 1982 before changing back.[[31]](#cite_note-Historical_Kits_East_Stirlingshire-31)

The first company to supply kits for East Stirlingshire was [Bukta](/wiki/Bukta "Bukta") between 1979 and 1987.[[31]](#cite_note-Historical_Kits_East_Stirlingshire-31) For the [1998–99 season](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_in_Scottish_football "1998–99 in Scottish football"), the club signed a deal with French kit supplier [Le Coq Sportif](/wiki/Le_Coq_Sportif "Le Coq Sportif") who failed to deliver the kits in time for the start of the season so sent a set of [Queen's Park](/wiki/Queen%27s_Park_F.C. "Queen's Park F.C.") strips with East Stirlingshire's crest and sponsors ironed on top of the original.[[31]](#cite_note-Historical_Kits_East_Stirlingshire-31) The supplier for the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_in_Scottish_football "2012–13 in Scottish football") is [Jako](/wiki/Jako "Jako"). Past suppliers include [Hummel](/wiki/Hummel_International "Hummel International"), [Umbro](/wiki/Umbro "Umbro") and [Joma](/wiki/Joma "Joma").[[31]](#cite_note-Historical_Kits_East_Stirlingshire-31)

The club crest is a shield with black and white stripes and a stylised football, with a banner displaying the club name at the top. It has several variations with the football and banner coloured black, white or orange in different versions.[[7]](#cite_note-East_Stirlingshire_SPFL-7)[[31]](#cite_note-Historical_Kits_East_Stirlingshire-31)[[49]](#cite_note-East_Stirlingshire_Store_Crests-49)

## Grounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Stirlingshire_F.C.&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Grounds")]

East Stirlingshire has played at several grounds over its history. In the club's early existence as *Britannia*, it played one of its first known matches at Burnhouse[[9]](#cite_note-Falkirk_FC_matches-9) against a [Falkirk 2nd XI](/wiki/Falkirk_F.C. "Falkirk F.C."), which ended in a 7–0 defeat for the team.[[8]](#cite_note-History_1880-1900-8) Burnhouse is thought to be the location that was to become Merchiston Park, where the club played many of its matches in the late 19th century.[[9]](#cite_note-Falkirk_FC_matches-9) Some home matches were also played at Randyford Park in the town.[[8]](#cite_note-History_1880-1900-8)

[![A football match with players and a referee on the pitch. A small spectator stand full of people is in the background.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Firs_Park.JPG/250px-Firs_Park.JPG)](/wiki/File:Firs_Park.JPG)

[Firs Park](/wiki/Firs_Park "Firs Park")'s final match, showing the main stand in the background

[Merchiston Park](/wiki/Merchiston_Park "Merchiston Park") was a football ground located in the village of [Bainsford](/wiki/Bainsford "Bainsford"), approximately 1 mile (1.6 km) north of [Falkirk](/wiki/Falkirk "Falkirk"). It was situated on the northern bank of the [Forth and Clyde Canal](/wiki/Forth_and_Clyde_Canal "Forth and Clyde Canal") near to present day Main Street in Bainsford.
The ground hosted East Stirlingshire's first [Scottish Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League "Scottish Football League") match in [1900–01](/wiki/1900%E2%80%9301_Scottish_Second_Division "1900–01 Scottish Second Division") which ended in a 3–2 defeat to [Airdrieonians](/wiki/Airdrieonians_F.C._(1878) "Airdrieonians F.C. (1878)").[[53]](#cite_note-History_1900-1940-53)[[54]](#cite_note-London_Hearts_1900-54) Merchiston Park remained the club's home until 1921 when a nearby iron works acquired the ground for expansion and the club moved to Firs Park.[[55]](#cite_note-Merchiston_1907-55)

[Firs Park](/wiki/Firs_Park "Firs Park") was located to the south of the canal in the centre of Falkirk, named after the street in which it was situated, Firs Street.[[2]](#cite_note-FH_Shire_Mettle-2) The club moved to the ground in 1921 and in the same year, the club's [record attendance](/wiki/Scottish_football_attendance_records "Scottish football attendance records") was set at a [Scottish Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Cup "Scottish Cup") match against [Partick Thistle](/wiki/Partick_Thistle_F.C. "Partick Thistle F.C.") when 12,000 spectators attended.[[56]](#cite_note-Record_attendance-56) Firs Park was East Stirlingshire's home for 87 years, except a solitary season in 1964 when the club played at [Kilbowie Park](/wiki/Kilbowie_Park "Kilbowie Park") in [Clydebank](/wiki/Clydebank "Clydebank") after being controversially merged with [Clydebank Juniors](/wiki/Clydebank_Juniors_F.C. "Clydebank Juniors F.C."). As a result of the merger, Firs Park was closed, however, after only one season, the merger was disbanded and East Stirlingshire moved back to the ground, where it remained until the end of the [2007–08 season](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_in_Scottish_football "2007–08 in Scottish football"). The decision was taken by the club to close Firs Park due to the prohibitive costs of potential refurbishing to meet new [Scottish Football Association](/wiki/Scottish_Football_Association "Scottish Football Association") stadium criteria.[[5]](#cite_note-Firs_Park_departure-5) At the time of closure, the ground had a capacity of 1,800 with 200 seated in the main stand.

After leaving Firs Park in 2008 they played their home matches at [Ochilview Park](/wiki/Ochilview_Park "Ochilview Park") the home of local rivals [Stenhousemuir](/wiki/Stenhousemuir_F.C. "Stenhousemuir F.C."); the club entered a [groundshare](/wiki/Groundshare "Groundshare") agreement with Stenhousemuir, originally intended to be for a period of five years, during which the club planned to develop a new stadium in Falkirk.[[5]](#cite_note-Firs_Park_departure-5)

Since the summer of 2018, East Stirlingshire have been playing their home matches at [Falkirk Stadium](/wiki/Falkirk_Stadium "Falkirk Stadium") which is the home ground of local rivals [Falkirk](/wiki/Falkirk_F.C. "Falkirk F.C.").[[57]](#cite_note-backin-57) In May 2014, the club entered into a partnership with LK Galaxy Sports and others to develop a new playing facility at the former BP Club site at Little Kerse, between [Grangemouth](/wiki/Grangemouth "Grangemouth") and [Polmont](/wiki/Polmont "Polmont").[[58]](#cite_note-58) The site would also host other sports in due course. Planning was approved by Falkirk Council, but did not proceed at the time due to a lack of funds. The club trained at Little Kerse until the 2018 switch to the Falkirk Stadium, when all operations were moved to that venue.[[57]](#cite_note-backin-57)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Stirlingshire_F.C.&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

See also: [East Stirlingshire F.C.–Falkirk F.C. rivalry](/wiki/East_Stirlingshire_F.C.%E2%80%93Falkirk_F.C._rivalry "East Stirlingshire F.C.–Falkirk F.C. rivalry")

East Stirlingshire's traditional rival is [Falkirk](/wiki/Falkirk_F.C. "Falkirk F.C.").[[59]](#cite_note-Real_Derby-59)[[60]](#cite_note-Scotsman_Dangerously-60) The club's first match known as both *Britannia* and *East Stirlingshire* were friendly games against Falkirk in 1880 and 1881. Both clubs are based in [Falkirk](/wiki/Falkirk "Falkirk") and have competed against each other in competitions such as [Stirlingshire Cup](/wiki/Stirlingshire_Cup "Stirlingshire Cup") since the late 19th century. East Stirlingshire was elected to the bottom tier of the Scottish Football League in 1900, closely followed by Falkirk two seasons later in [1902–03](/wiki/1902%E2%80%9303_in_Scottish_football "1902–03 in Scottish football"). The first competitive league meeting between the clubs ended in a 2–0 win for Falkirk at [Merchiston Park](/wiki/Merchiston_Park "Merchiston Park") in August 1902.[[61]](#cite_note-Statto_H2H_Falkirk-61) The two clubs' old grounds of [Firs Park](/wiki/Firs_Park "Firs Park") and [Brockville Park](/wiki/Brockville_Park "Brockville Park") were geographically close, separated by less than half a mile (0.8 km).

On a regional scale, East Stirlingshire has rivalries with football clubs from the historical county of [Stirlingshire](/wiki/Stirlingshire "Stirlingshire"), who have competed in the [Stirlingshire Cup](/wiki/Stirlingshire_Cup "Stirlingshire Cup") against each other since the early 1880s such as [Stenhousemuir](/wiki/Stenhousemuir_F.C. "Stenhousemuir F.C.")[[62]](#cite_note-Stenhousemuir_v_Shire-62)[[63]](#cite_note-FH_Derby_Stenny_Shire-63) and since 1945, [Stirling Albion](/wiki/Stirling_Albion_F.C. "Stirling Albion F.C.")[[64]](#cite_note-Stirling_doubt-64)[[65]](#cite_note-Stirlingshire_derby_STV-65) and [Alloa Athletic](/wiki/Alloa_Athletic_F.C. "Alloa Athletic F.C.").[[66]](#cite_note-Herald_Numbers_Game-66) East Stirlingshire played its home matches in a [ground-share](/wiki/Groundshare "Groundshare") agreement with rivals Stenhousemuir at their [Ochilview Park](/wiki/Ochilview_Park "Ochilview Park") home until 2018.

## Notable players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Stirlingshire_F.C.&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Notable players")]

Main article: [List of East Stirlingshire F.C. players](/wiki/List_of_East_Stirlingshire_F.C._players "List of East Stirlingshire F.C. players")

For a list of all East Stirlingshire players with a Wikipedia article, see [Category:East Stirlingshire F.C. players](/wiki/Category:East_Stirlingshire_F.C._players "Category:East Stirlingshire F.C. players").

Gordon Russell holds the record for the most number of [Scottish Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League "Scottish Football League") appearances for East Stirlingshire with 415 between 1983 and 2002. He also went on to become the manager of the club for a short period in 2002.[[68]](#cite_note-Gordon_Russell_apps-68)[[69]](#cite_note-Cult_heroes_BBC-69)

East Stirlingshire players have been [capped](/wiki/Cap_(sport) "Cap (sport)") for their country at full international level 9 times by four players. The first person to do so was [Humphrey Jones](/wiki/Humphrey_Jones "Humphrey Jones"), who captained the [Wales national team](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") four times out of the five caps he earned whilst at East Stirlingshire. His first came in a [British Home Championship](/wiki/British_Home_Championship "British Home Championship") match against [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") in [1889](/wiki/1888%E2%80%9389_British_Home_Championship "1888–89 British Home Championship").[[70]](#cite_note-Jones_Shire_Debut-70)[[71]](#cite_note-WalesVEngland_Jones-71) The three other players to make an appearance for their country are [Archibald Ritchie](/wiki/Archibald_Ritchie_(footballer,_born_1872) "Archibald Ritchie (footballer, born 1872)"), [David Alexander](/wiki/David_Alexander_(footballer) "David Alexander (footballer)") and [James McKee](/wiki/James_McKee_(footballer) "James McKee (footballer)"), all for the [Scotland national team](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") at the British Championship. Each of them made their début against Wales, although in different seasons. Archibald Ritchie's one and only Scotland cap came in March [1891](/wiki/1890%E2%80%9391_British_Home_Championship "1890–91 British Home Championship") against Wales,[[72]](#cite_note-Ritchie_London_Hearts-72) who were captained by former East Stirlingshire player Humphrey Jones.[[73]](#cite_note-Ritchie_Shire_Debut-73) David Alexander made two appearances, one against [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_football_team_(1882%E2%80%931950) "Ireland national football team (1882–1950)") and scoring against Wales at the [1894 British Home Championship](/wiki/1893%E2%80%9394_British_Home_Championship "1893–94 British Home Championship") which Scotland won.[[74]](#cite_note-Alexander_London_Hearts-74) James McKee scored twice during his only appearance for Scotland, in a 5–2 victory over Wales in [1898](/wiki/1897%E2%80%9398_British_Home_Championship "1897–98 British Home Championship"). He is the last East Stirlingshire player to be capped by his country. Murray Brown was a steadfast defender during the late 90s having performed to a high standard at West Bromwich Albion during his teenage years.[[75]](#cite_note-McKie_London_Hearts-75)

## Notable managers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Stirlingshire_F.C.&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Notable managers")]

Main article: [List of East Stirlingshire F.C. managers](/wiki/List_of_East_Stirlingshire_F.C._managers "List of East Stirlingshire F.C. managers")

[![A portrait of Alex Ferguson.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Alex_Ferguson_02.jpg/250px-Alex_Ferguson_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alex_Ferguson_02.jpg)

[Alex Ferguson](/wiki/Alex_Ferguson "Alex Ferguson")'s first managerial role was at East Stirlingshire in 1974.

East Stirlingshire did not officially appoint a manager until 1966. Before then, all management decisions were taken by the board of directors at the club. The first person to manage the club was Lawrence Binnie in 1966.[[28]](#cite_note-History_1940-1970-28) The club's most famous manager is former [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") manager, [Sir Alex Ferguson](/wiki/Alex_Ferguson "Alex Ferguson"), who in his first position as manager, stayed at East Stirlingshire during 1974 before moving to [St Mirren](/wiki/St_Mirren_F.C. "St Mirren F.C.").[[36]](#cite_note-History_1970-2000-36)

No manager has won a league title with the club, however, [Billy Lamont](/wiki/Billy_Lamont "Billy Lamont") managed East Stirlingshire to promotion to the [1980–81 Scottish First Division](/wiki/1980%E2%80%9381_Scottish_First_Division "1980–81 Scottish First Division") after finishing runners-up in Division Two, one point behind rivals [Falkirk](/wiki/Falkirk_F.C. "Falkirk F.C."). He also took East Stirlingshire to the quarter-finals of the [Scottish Cup](/wiki/1980%E2%80%9381_Scottish_Cup "1980–81 Scottish Cup"), the furthest stage of the tournament the club has reached since reaching the same stage 91 years beforehand in [1889–90](/wiki/1889%E2%80%9390_Scottish_Cup "1889–90 Scottish Cup").[[36]](#cite_note-History_1970-2000-36)

After five consecutive seasons finishing bottom of the [Scottish Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League "Scottish Football League") in the mid-2000s, [Jim McInally](/wiki/Jim_McInally "Jim McInally") also changed fortunes at the club, managing East Stirlingshire to successive third-place finishes in the [Third Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Third_Division "Scottish Football League Third Division") between 2008 and 2010 to qualify for the [promotion play-offs](/wiki/Playoffs#Scotland "Playoffs").[[76]](#cite_note-Jim_McInally_BBC-76)

## Records and statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Stirlingshire_F.C.&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Records and statistics")]

Further information: [List of East Stirlingshire F.C. records and statistics](/wiki/List_of_East_Stirlingshire_F.C._records_and_statistics "List of East Stirlingshire F.C. records and statistics")

[![refer to caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/86/East_Stirlingshire_FC_average_league_attendances_1900_to_2012.png/330px-East_Stirlingshire_FC_average_league_attendances_1900_to_2012.png)](/wiki/File:East_Stirlingshire_FC_average_league_attendances_1900_to_2012.png)

Chart showing East Stirlingshire's average home league attendance from 1900 to 2012

The club's record Scottish Football League victory is 8–0 against [Arthurlie](/wiki/Arthurlie_F.C. "Arthurlie F.C.") in August 1927 in [Division Two](/wiki/1927%E2%80%9328_Scottish_Second_Division "1927–28 Scottish Second Division") and its record defeat is 12–1 to [Dundee United](/wiki/Dundee_United_F.C. "Dundee United F.C.") in April 1936 in the same division.[[20]](#cite_note-statto_records-20) In the Scottish Cup, the club's record winning margin is 9 goals which it achieved in consecutive rounds of the [1888–89 Scottish Cup](/wiki/1888%E2%80%9389_Scottish_Cup "1888–89 Scottish Cup") tournament: 10–1 against [Stenhousemuir](/wiki/Stenhousemuir_F.C. "Stenhousemuir F.C.") in the first round on 1 September 1888 and 11–2 against [Vale of Bannock](/wiki/Vale_of_Bannock_F.C. "Vale of Bannock F.C.") in the second round on 22 September 1888.[[20]](#cite_note-statto_records-20) In only its second season of competing in the cup, East Stirlingshire recorded its greatest loss: 2–10 to [Renton](/wiki/Renton_F.C. "Renton F.C.") in October 1884.[[20]](#cite_note-statto_records-20)

East Stirlingshire's [record home attendance](/wiki/Scottish_football_attendance_records "Scottish football attendance records") is 12,000 for a third round Scottish Cup match against [Partick Thistle](/wiki/Partick_Thistle_F.C. "Partick Thistle F.C.") on 19 February 1921 at [Firs Park](/wiki/Firs_Park "Firs Park").[[a]](#cite_note-83)

Gordon Russell holds the record for East Stirlingshire league appearances, playing 445 first-team matches between [1983–84](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_in_Scottish_football "1983–84 in Scottish football") and [2000–01](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_in_Scottish_football "2000–01 in Scottish football").[[69]](#cite_note-Cult_heroes_BBC-69) The record for most league goals in a single season is 41 by [Andy Rodgers](/wiki/Andy_Rodgers "Andy Rodgers") in the [2016–17 Lowland Football League](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Lowland_Football_League "2016–17 Lowland Football League"). [Humphrey Jones](/wiki/Humphrey_Jones "Humphrey Jones") holds the record number of international [caps](/wiki/Cap_(sport) "Cap (sport)") earned as an East Stirlingshire player with 5 for the [Wales national football team](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") between 1889 and 1890.

The highest transfer fee received for an East Stirlingshire player is [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")35,000 from English club [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") for [Jim Docherty](/wiki/Jim_Docherty "Jim Docherty") in 1978,[[69]](#cite_note-Cult_heroes_BBC-69) while the most paid by the club is the compensation development fee paid to [Spartans](/wiki/Spartans_F.C. "Spartans F.C.") for Jamie Dishington. The amount was higher than the previous record, £6,000 for the transfer of Colin McKinnon from [Falkirk](/wiki/Falkirk_F.C. "Falkirk F.C.") in 1991.[[83]](#cite_note-84)
